candidate_problems.text: How can 'used as stand-ins for distal structure' be made non-circular and operationally testable? The conjecture relies on internal states being genuine surrogates rather than mere correlations, but it leaves open a criterion for distinguishing representation from any causally efficacious latent variable in a control system. A key open problem is to specify necessary and sufficient conditions—interventionally, informationally, or algorithmically—under which a state counts as about X rather than merely helping produce adaptive behavior.

Is computational irreducibility actually necessary for intentionality, or only one route to explanatory indispensability? The conjecture ties aboutness to cases where no compact shortcut from physics to behavior exists, but it remains unresolved whether reducible systems could still possess genuine intentional states, or whether irreducibility is doing too much metaphysical work. This raises a sharp problem: determine whether intentionality tracks irreducibility itself, bounded rationality under local information, model-based control more generally, or some other structural feature.

What principled metric determines when 'treating a state as about X is part of the shortest adequate explanation' across levels of description? The conjecture appeals to explanatory compression, but adequacy and shortestness are model-relative and observer-sensitive. An important unresolved tension is whether intentionality can be grounded in an objective complexity measure—e.g., intervention cost, predictive efficiency, minimum description length, or causal abstraction—or whether the view collapses into pragmatism about explanation.
